algorithms.title: Categorizing Pediatric Respiratory Distress and Failure
algorithms.description: A systematic approach to classifying respiratory compromise in children into the four pathophysiologic categories based on clinical assessment, enabling rapid selection of appropriate first-line treatment.
steps: Perform rapid primary assessment using the Pediatric Assessment Triangle: appearance, work of breathing, and circulation to skin.
Auscultate the airway and lungs: listen for stridor (inspiratory), wheeze (expiratory), or crackles (rales).

If inspiratory stridor is present → Upper Airway Obstruction.
  • Barking cough + hoarseness → Croup → dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg PO/IM + nebulized racemic epinephrine.
  • Drooling + tripod + muffled voice → Epiglottitis → do not agitate; call airway team immediately.
  • Sudden onset + choking history + asymmetric breath sounds → Foreign body → rigid bronchoscopy.

If expiratory wheezing with prolonged expiration is present → Lower Airway Obstruction.
  • Diffuse wheezing → Asthma/Bronchiolitis → nebulized albuterol 2.5 mg q20 min × 3, supplemental O2.
  • Assess for severity: ability to speak, retractions, SpO2 response to initial bronchodilators.

If crackles/rales are present with hypoxemia and radiographic infiltrates → Lung Tissue Disease.
  • Pneumonia → antibiotics within 1 hour + supplemental O2.
  • Pulmonary edema → diuretics, oxygen, identify cardiac vs non-cardiac cause.
  • ARDS → lung-protective ventilation, treat underlying cause.

If respiratory pattern is abnormal (irregular, apneic, Cheyne-Stokes, shallow) with clear lungs → Disordered Control of Breathing.
  • Neurological cause → secure the airway, provide mechanical ventilation.
  • Neuromuscular cause → anticipate progressive ventilatory failure, plan for long-term support.
  • Drug toxicity (opioids) → naloxone if opioid-induced; support ventilation.

Reassess continuously: any worsening despite category-appropriate treatment signals progression toward respiratory failure — escalate to positive-pressure ventilation and prepare for advanced airway management.
Remember: infants and small children decompensate rapidly. Do not wait for dramatic deterioration before intervening — act on early signs of distress.
